1: A direct: "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." The peace is not a feeling but a covenantal status — the end of the enmity (cf. Romans 8:7, the flesh hostile to God) achieved through the mediatorial work of Christ. Peace-with-God is the first-named consequence of the justification Paul has been establishing.
2: A direct: "We have gained access by faith into this grace in which we now stand." Prosagōgē (access) is a court-and-temple term: the right to come before the sovereign. Christ provides this access (Ephesians 2:18; Romans 3:12) where the law could not. Boasting in hope of glory reverses the shame of Romans 3:23 ("falling short of the glory of God") with eschatological confidence.
3: A theme: "We also glory in our sufferings, because we know that suffering produces perseverance." The paradox of finding ground for confidence in suffering rather than in achievement is the ethic of those who have been reconciled by Christ's death when they were enemies (Romans 5:10). The suffering-to-glory sequence parallels Christ's own path (Romans 8:17).
4: A theme: Perseverance produces character, and character hope. The virtuous sequence of Romans 5:3-4 is the outworking in the believer of what Christ's resurrection established: death does not have the final word; suffering is the seedbed of life. The hope it produces does not disappoint because it rests on the same God who raised Christ.
5: A direct: "God's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us." The Spirit poured out is the fulfillment of Joel 2:28-29 and Ezekiel 36:26-27; the vehicle of this love is the Spirit given because of Christ's atoning work (cf. Galatians 4:4-6). The love poured in is the experiential confirmation of the reconciliation announced in Romans 5:1-2.
6: A direct: "You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly." The timing (kairō = the appointed time) grounds the cross in divine sovereign purpose, not contingency. The recipients — "powerless," "ungodly" — define the scandalous direction of Christ's self-giving: not toward the deserving but toward those with no claim whatsoever.
7: A direct: "Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person someone might possibly dare to die." Paul argues by analogy from human experience: even the most heroic human death for the worthy is exceptional. Christ's death for enemies (Romans 5:10) and the ungodly (Romans 5:6) is of an entirely different category — which is why it reveals and demonstrates the love of God rather than merely human virtue.
8: A direct: "But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us." The demonstration (synistēsin = commends, proves by proof) is an objective act of God in history, not a feeling. The Christological claim is absolute: Christ's death is God's own self-demonstration of love — identifying Christ's act with God's own action.
9: A direct: "Since we have now been justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved from God's wrath through him!" The a fortiori argument: if the more difficult thing (justification of sinners) has been accomplished, the less difficult (deliverance from wrath at the final judgment) is secure. The blood — the sacrificial offering language — is the basis of present justification and future deliverance.
10: A direct: "For if, while we were God's enemies, we were reconciled to him through the death of his Son, how much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved through his life!" Reconciliation through Christ's death and salvation through his resurrected life are the two phases of the same redemption. The living Christ (not only the dying Christ) is Paul's ground of confidence for final salvation.
11: A direct: "We also boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation." The mediation is explicit: all access to God, all basis for confident relationship with God, comes through (dia) Jesus Christ. Boasting in God through Christ is the eschatological inversion of boasting in self-achievement.
12: A type: "Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned..." The Adam-narrative begins. Paul's argument in Romans 5:12-21 establishes Adam as the type whose action determined the condition of all who came after him — by analogy with and contrast to Christ, whose action determines the condition of all in him.
13: A theme: "To be sure, sin was in the world before the law was given, but sin is not charged against anyone's account where there is no law." The pre-law period demonstrates that death reigned independently of legal condemnation — which establishes that the death-problem is deeper than legal transgression and requires more than law-keeping to resolve.
14: A type: "Nevertheless, death reigned from the time of Adam to the time of Moses, even over those who did not sin by breaking a command, as did Adam, who is a pattern of the one to come." Paul explicitly names Adam as a type (typos) of Christ. The structural correspondence: one man's act determines the condition of all — in Adam, all die; in Christ, all (who are in him) are made alive. This is not a reader-imposed analogy; it is Paul's own typological argument.
15: A type: "But the gift is not like the trespass. For if the many died by the trespass of the one man, how much more did God's grace and the gift that came by the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, overflow to the many!" The gift far exceeds the trespass in both quantity (perisseuō = overflow) and quality (grace versus condemnation). The type-and-antitype relationship is asymmetrical: Christ's act is infinitely more productive than Adam's was destructive.
16: A type: "Nor can the gift of God be compared with the result of one man's sin: The judgment followed one sin and brought condemnation, but the gift followed many trespasses and brought justification." The economy of divine grace inverts the economy of sin: one sin brought one condemnation; many sins brought one justification. The multiplication of sin does not outpace the gift.
17: A direct: "For if, by the trespass of the one man, death reigned through that one man, how much more will those who receive God's abundant provision of grace and of the gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man, Jesus Christ!" The reign of life through Christ explicitly answers the reign of death through Adam. The two-realm structure (death's reign vs. life's reign) is the Christological frame for the entire passage.
18: A direct: "Consequently, just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people." The parallel is exact: one act, universal consequence. The "one righteous act" (dikaiōma) is Christ's atoning obedience, which Paul specifies in the next verse as his "obedience." This is the gospel's claim in its most compressed form.
19: A direct: "For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The "obedience of Christ" is the active dimension of his life and death that accomplishes what Adam's disobedience undid. This is the basis of what later theology called Christ's active obedience — not merely dying for sin but living the perfect obedience that constitutes righteousness for all in him.
20: A theme: "The law was brought in so that the trespass might increase. But where sin increased, grace increased all the more." The law's role as sin-multiplier (by defining and therefore increasing transgression) is the penultimate word; grace's super-abundance is the final word. The law drives toward the need for grace; grace drives toward Christ who fulfills the law.
21: A direct: "So that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord." The final verse of the Adam/Christ argument closes the comparison: grace reigns through righteousness (Christ's own righteousness imputed and imparted), and its destination is eternal life — not death. The mediatorial phrase "through Jesus Christ our Lord" anchors the entire economy of grace in the specific person of the risen Lord.
